title: Cape May, New Jersey

infobox.value: 341st of 565 in state 8th of 16 in county[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)

(sometimes **Cape May City**) is a [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") and [seaside resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") located at the southern tip of [Cape May Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_May_Peninsula "Cape May Peninsula") in [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). Located on the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") near the mouth of the [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"), it is one of the country's oldest vacation resort destinations.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The city, and all of Cape May County, is part of the [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_New_Jersey "Ocean City, New Jersey") [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), and is part of the [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")-[Wilmington](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware")-[Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey"), [PA](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")-NJ-[DE](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware")-[MD](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area"), also known as the [Delaware Valley or Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley").[[20]](#cite_note-20) It is the [southernmost municipality](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_U.S._states "List of extreme points of U.S. states") in New Jersey.

by the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on March 8, 1848, from portions of [Lower Township](/wiki/Lower_Township,_New_Jersey "Lower Township, New Jersey"). It was reincorporated as **Cape Island City** on March 10, 1851, and was renamed Cape May City on March 9, 1869.[[33]](#cite_note-Story-33)

**Cape May** (sometimes **Cape May City**) is a [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") and [seaside resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort") located at the southern tip of [Cape May Peninsula](/wiki/Cape_May_Peninsula "Cape May Peninsula") in [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). Located on the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") near the mouth of the [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"), it is one of the country's oldest vacation resort destinations.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The city, and all of Cape May County, is part of the [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_New_Jersey "Ocean City, New Jersey") [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area"), and is part of the [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")-[Wilmington](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware")-[Camden](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey"), [PA](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")-NJ-[DE](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware")-[MD](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area"), also known as the [Delaware Valley or Philadelphia metropolitan area](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley").[[20]](#cite_note-20) It is the [southernmost municipality](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_U.S._states "List of extreme points of U.S. states") in New Jersey.

As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the city's resident population was 2,768,[[21]](#cite_note-Census2020-21)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) a decrease of 839 (−23.3%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 3,607,[[22]](#cite_note-Census2010-22)[[23]](#cite_note-LWD2010-23) which in turn reflected a decline of 427 (−10.6%) from the 4,034 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In the summer, Cape May's population is expanded by as many as 40,000 to 50,000 visitors.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-NYT1989-26) The entire city of Cape May is designated the [Cape May Historic District](/wiki/Cape_May_Historic_District "Cape May Historic District"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") due to its concentration of [Victorian architecture](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture").

In 2008, Cape May was recognized as one of the top 10 beaches in the United States by the [Travel Channel](/wiki/Travel_Channel "Travel Channel").[[27]](#cite_note-27) It is part of the [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") region of the state.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 17th and 18th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 17th and 18th centuries")]

*A Stroll along Beach Avenue, Cape May, New Jersey* (Video 3:35)

The area that is now Cape May was originally inhabited by the [Kechemeche](/wiki/Kechemeche "Kechemeche") [Native American tribe](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), a subgroup of the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") people.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Kechemeche first encountered Europeans around 1600.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city takes its name from the [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") captain [Cornelius Jacobsen Mey](/wiki/Cornelius_Jacobsen_Mey "Cornelius Jacobsen Mey"), who charted the coast between 1611 and 1614 and claimed it for the province of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland").[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) It was later settled by [New Englanders](/wiki/New_England "New England") from the [New Haven Colony](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony").

Cape May began hosting vacationers from [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") in the mid-18th century and is recognized as the country's oldest [seaside resort](/wiki/Seaside_resort "Seaside resort").[[31]](#cite_note-31)
[[26]](#cite_note-NYT1989-26)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Following the construction of [Congress Hall](/wiki/Congress_Hall_(Cape_May_hotel) "Congress Hall (Cape May hotel)") in 1816, Cape May became increasingly popular in the 19th century and was considered one of the finest resorts in America by the 20th century.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

What is now Cape May was formed as the [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_Jersey) "Borough (New Jersey)") of **Cape Island** by the [New Jersey Legislature](/wiki/New_Jersey_Legislature "New Jersey Legislature") on March 8, 1848, from portions of [Lower Township](/wiki/Lower_Township,_New_Jersey "Lower Township, New Jersey"). It was reincorporated as **Cape Island City** on March 10, 1851, and was renamed Cape May City on March 9, 1869.[[33]](#cite_note-Story-33)

Tourism to the city was boosted in 1863 with the opening of the [Tuckahoe and Cape May Railroad](/wiki/Atlantic_City_Railroad "Atlantic City Railroad").

The city suffered devastating fires in 1869 and 1878. In the early hours of August 31, 1869, a fire broke out in the Japanese store on Washington Street. The fire destroyed the post office and at least thirty-five other buildings. Press reports at the time did not mention any deaths.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In 1878, a five-day-long fire destroyed 30 blocks of the town center. Replacement homes were almost uniformly of Victorian style,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and more recent protectionist efforts have left Cape May with many famously well-maintained [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") houses—the second largest collection of such homes in the nation after San Francisco[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Cape May City Athletic Association was founded in 1886 and opened the [Cape May Athletic Park](/wiki/Cape_May_Athletic_Park "Cape May Athletic Park") in 1887. Cape May attracted the Philadelphia Phillies who held spring training in Cape May at the Athletic Park in 1888, the Gas House field in 1891, and again at the Athletic Park in 1898.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The Athletic Association fielded a collegiate baseball team that played every summer against other local clubs, as well as semi-professional and professional teams including African-American and Major League teams. President [Benjamin Harrison](/wiki/Benjamin_Harrison "Benjamin Harrison") visited Cape May in August 1891 and visited the Athletic Park where he saw Cape May lose 5 to 0 to the Gorham African-American club.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Because of the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") submarine threat off the [East Coast of the United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), especially off the shore of Cape May and at the mouth of the [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"), numerous [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") facilities were located here in order to protect American coastal shipping. Cape May Naval facilities, listed below, provided significant help in reducing the number of ships and crew members lost at sea.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

* Naval Air Station, Cape May
* Naval Base, Cape May
* Inshore Patrol, Cape May
* Naval Annex, Inshore Patrol, Cape May
* Joint Operations Office, Naval Base, Cape May
* Welfare and Recreation Office, Cape May
* Dispensary, Naval Air Station, Cape May
* Naval Frontier Base, Cape May
* Degaussing Range (Cold Spring Inlet), Naval Base, Cape May
* Joint Operations Office, Commander Delaware Group, ESF, Cape May
* Anti-Submarine Attack Teacher Training Unit, U.S. Naval Base, Cape May
* Naval Annex, Admiral Hotel, Cape May

In 1976, Cape May was designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") as the [Cape May Historic District](/wiki/Cape_May_Historic_District "Cape May Historic District"), making Cape May the only city in the U.S. to be wholly designated as a national historic district.[[39]](#cite_note-39) That designation is intended to ensure the architectural preservation of these buildings.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Cape_May_Harbor.jpg/330px-Cape_May_Harbor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Harbor.jpg)

Cape May Harbor as seen from Devil's Reach

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Emlen-physick-estate.jpg/330px-Emlen-physick-estate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emlen-physick-estate.jpg)

The [Emlen Physick Estate](/wiki/Emlen_Physick_Estate "Emlen Physick Estate") in Cape May in 1879. [Frank Furness](/wiki/Frank_Furness "Frank Furness") was the architect of this [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") house, which is now a museum located at 1048 Washington Street

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Cape May had a total area of 2.90 square miles (7.50 km2), including 2.47 square miles (6.41 km2) of land and 0.42 square miles (1.10 km2) of water (14.59%).[[1]](#cite_note-CensusArea-1)[[2]](#cite_note-GR1-2) Cape May is generally low-lying; its highest point, at the intersection of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Cape_May) "Washington Street (Cape May)") and Jackson Streets, is 14 ft (4.3 m) above sea level.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[Unincorporated communities](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Jersey#Unincorporated_communities "Local government in New Jersey"), localities and place names located partially or completely within the city include Poverty Beach.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Cape May borders the Cape May County municipalities of [Lower Township](/wiki/Lower_Township,_New_Jersey "Lower Township, New Jersey") and [West Cape May Borough](/wiki/West_Cape_May,_New_Jersey "West Cape May, New Jersey") and the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) The [Cape May–Lewes Ferry](/wiki/Cape_May%E2%80%93Lewes_Ferry "Cape May–Lewes Ferry") provides transportation across the Delaware Bay between [North Cape May, New Jersey](/wiki/North_Cape_May,_New_Jersey "North Cape May, New Jersey"), and [Lewes, Delaware](/wiki/Lewes,_Delaware "Lewes, Delaware").

Cape May Harbor, which borders Lower Township and nearby [Wildwood Crest](/wiki/Wildwood_Crest,_New_Jersey "Wildwood Crest, New Jersey") allows fishing vessels to enter from the Atlantic Ocean, was created as of 1911, after years of dredging completed the harbor which covers 500 acres (200 ha).[[45]](#cite_note-45) Cape May Harbor Fest celebrates life in and around the harbor, with the 2011 event commemorating the 100th anniversary of the harbor's creation.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Cape May is the southernmost point in New Jersey.[[47]](#cite_note-47) It is at approximately the same latitude as [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and [Arlington County, Virginia](/wiki/Arlington_County,_Virginia "Arlington County, Virginia"), and equidistant to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia_Beach,_Virginia "Virginia Beach, Virginia").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") system, Cape May has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) with hot, humid summers, cool winters and year-round precipitation. Its climate resembles that of its neighbor, the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula"). During the summer months in Cape May, a cooling afternoon [sea breeze](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze") is present on most days, but episodes of extreme heat and humidity can occur with [heat index](/wiki/Heat_index "Heat index") values at or above 95.0 °F (35.0 °C). During the winter months, episodes of extreme cold and wind can occur with [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill") values 0.0 °F (−17.8 °C). The [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") of Cape May is 8a with an average annual extreme minimum air temperature of 10.8 °F (−11.8 °C). The average seasonal snowfall total is around 15 in (380 mm), and the average snowiest month is February which corresponds with the annual peak in [nor'easter](/wiki/Nor%27easter "Nor'easter") activity.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 3,607 people, 1,457 households, and 782 families in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,500.6 per square mile (579.4/km2). There were 4,155 housing units at an average density of 1,728.5 per square mile (667.4/km2). The racial makeup was 89.05% (3,212) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 4.85% (175) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.30% (11) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 0.67% (24) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.11% (4) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 2.30% (83) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 2.72% (98) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 8.62% (311) of the population.[[22]](#cite_note-Census2010-22)

Of the 1,457 households, 16.3% had children under the age of 18; 44.6% were married couples living together; 7.5% had a female householder with no husband present and 46.3% were non-families. Of all households, 42.0% were made up of individuals and 27.9% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 1.95 and the average family size was 2.64.[[22]](#cite_note-Census2010-22)

12.8% of the population were under the age of 18, 20.6% from 18 to 24, 18.6% from 25 to 44, 20.3% from 45 to 64, and 27.6% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 42.2 years. For every 100 females, the population had 104.7 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 107.4 males.[[22]](#cite_note-Census2010-22)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $35,660 (with a margin of error of +/− $4,248) and the median family income was $50,846 (+/− $16,315). Males had a median income of $43,015 (+/− $20,953) versus $31,630 (+/− $22,691) for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $30,046 (+/− $4,010). About 2.2% of families and 4.8% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 5.1% of those under age 18 and 7.1% of those age 65 or over.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Washington_Street_Mall_EB_past_Decatur_Street.jpeg/250px-Washington_Street_Mall_EB_past_Decatur_Street.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Washington_Street_Mall_EB_past_Decatur_Street.jpeg)

Shops at the Washington Street Mall in Downtown Cape May

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Cape_May_Welcome_Sign.jpg/250px-Cape_May_Welcome_Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Welcome_Sign.jpg)

Cape May Welcome Sign

Tourism is Cape May's largest industry. The economy runs on the [Washington Street Mall](/wiki/Washington_Street_Mall "Washington Street Mall") and includes shops, restaurants, lodgings, and tourist attractions including the Cape May boardwalk. Many historic hotels and [B&Bs](/wiki/Bed_and_breakfast "Bed and breakfast") are located in Cape May, and commercial and sport fishing is a significant component of its economy.

Cove Beach, located at Cape May southernmost tip, hosts hundreds of swimmers, sunbathers, surfers, and hikers daily during summer months.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Cape May has been a popular resort for [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") tourists for several decades. [Cape May County](/wiki/Cape_May_County,_New_Jersey "Cape May County, New Jersey") established a tourism office in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), but around 1995 it closed due to budget cuts. By 2010, the tourism office of Cape May County established a [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language") coupon booklet.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Prior to the 1980s, many temporary seasonal workers originated from the Philadelphia and, to a lesser extent, the New York City areas. That decade, there had been a wave of temporary workers from Ireland. By 2004, an improvement in the Irish economy meant that not as many Irish people came in that role, and that many of the temporary workers were now from Eastern Europe.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Harriet_Tubman_Museum%2C_Cape_May%2C_NJ_-_2024.jpg/250px-Harriet_Tubman_Museum%2C_Cape_May%2C_NJ_-_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harriet_Tubman_Museum,_Cape_May,_NJ_-_2024.jpg)

The [Harriet Tubman Museum](/wiki/Harriet_Tubman_Museum "Harriet Tubman Museum") in Cape May

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Cape_May_Rotary_Park.jpg/250px-Cape_May_Rotary_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Rotary_Park.jpg)

Cape May Rotary Park

Cape May has become known both for its [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") [gingerbread homes](/wiki/Gingerbread_(architecture) "Gingerbread (architecture)") and its cultural offerings. The town hosts the Cape May Jazz Festival,[[67]](#cite_note-67) the Cape May Music Festival[[68]](#cite_note-68) and the Cape May, New Jersey Film Festival.[[69]](#cite_note-69) [Cape May Stage](/wiki/Cape_May_Stage "Cape May Stage"), an Equity theater founded in 1988, performs at the Robert Shackleton Playhouse on the corner of Bank and Lafayette Streets.[[70]](#cite_note-70) East Lynne Theater Company, an Equity professional company specializing in American classics and world premieres, has its mainstage season from June–December and March, with school residencies throughout the year.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Cape May is home to the Mid-Atlantic Center for the Arts & Humanities (MAC), established in 1970 by volunteers who succeeded in saving the 1879 [Emlen Physick Estate](/wiki/Emlen_Physick_Estate "Emlen Physick Estate") from demolition. MAC offers a wide variety of tours, activities and events throughout the year for residents and visitors and operates three Cape May area historic sites—the 1879 Emlen Physick Estate, the [Cape May Lighthouse](/wiki/Cape_May_Lighthouse "Cape May Lighthouse") and the World War II Lookout Tower.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The Center for Community Arts (CCA) offers African American history tours of Cape May, arts programs for young people[[73]](#cite_note-73) and is transforming the historic Franklin Street School, constructed in 1928 to house African-American students in a segregated school, into a Community Cultural Center.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

Cape May is the home of [Cape May diamonds](/wiki/Cape_May_diamonds "Cape May diamonds"), which show up at Sunset Beach and other beaches in the area. These are in fact clear [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz") pebbles that wash down from the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"). They begin as prismatic quartz (including the color sub-varieties such as smoky quartz and amethyst) in the quartz veins alongside the Delaware River that get eroded out of the host rock and wash down 200 miles to the shore. Collecting Cape May diamonds is a popular pastime and many tourist shops sell them polished or even as faceted stones.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The Cape May area is also world-famous for the observation of [migrating birds](/wiki/Bird_migration "Bird migration"), especially in the fall. With over 400 bird species having been recorded in this area by hundreds of local birders, Cape May is arguably the top bird-watching area in the entire Northeastern United States. The [Cape May Warbler](/wiki/Cape_May_Warbler "Cape May Warbler"), a small songbird, takes it name from this location. The [Cape May Bird Observatory](/wiki/Cape_May_Bird_Observatory "Cape May Bird Observatory") is based nearby at [Cape May Point](/wiki/Cape_May_Point,_New_Jersey "Cape May Point, New Jersey").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Cape May is also a destination for marine mammal watching. Several species of whales and dolphins can be seen in the waters of the Delaware Bay and Atlantic Ocean, many within 10 mi (16 km) of land, due to the confluence of fresh and saltwater that make for a nutrient rich area for marine life. Whale and dolphin watching cruises are a year-round attraction in Cape May, part of an ecotourism / agritourism industry that generated $450 million in revenue in the county, the most of any in the state.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The [Harriet Tubman Museum](/wiki/Harriet_Tubman_Museum "Harriet Tubman Museum") in downtown Cape May features the life and work of [Harriet Tubman](/wiki/Harriet_Tubman "Harriet Tubman"), an [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") and [social activist](/wiki/Social_activist "Social activist").[[78]](#cite_note-Museum-78)

### Fisherman's Memorial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Fisherman's Memorial")]

Cape May Fisherman's Memorial, located at Baltimore and Missouri Avenues, was built in 1988. It features a circular plaza reminiscent of a giant compass, a granite statue of a mother and two small children looking out to Harbor Cove, and a granite monument listing the names of 75 local fishermen who died at sea. The names begin with Andrew Jeffers, who died in 1893, and include the six people who died in March 2009 with the sinking of the scalloping boat *Lady Mary*.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The granite statue was designed by Heather Baird with Jerry Lynch. The memorial is maintained by the City of Cape May and administered by the Friends of the Cape May Fisherman's Memorial. Visitors often leave a stone or seashell on the statue's base in tribute to the fishermen.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Local government")]

Effective July 1, 2004, Cape May switched to a [Council-Manager](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(council%E2%80%93manager) "Faulkner Act (council–manager)") form of government under the [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act"),[[81]](#cite_note-81) after having used Plan A of the Faulkner Act [Small Municipality](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(small_municipality) "Faulkner Act (small municipality)") form since 1995.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) The city is one of 42 municipalities (of the 564) statewide that use this form of government.[[84]](#cite_note-84) The governing body is comprised of the Mayor and the four-member City Council, with all positions elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") to four-year terms of office on a [non-partisan](/wiki/Non-partisan_democracy "Non-partisan democracy") basis as part of the November general election in even-numbered years. The Mayor is elected [directly](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election") by the voters. The Borough Council is elected to serve four-year terms on a staggered basis, with three seats coming up for election together and then the mayor and the fourth council seat up for vote together two years later. Following the 2004 elections, the first under the new form of government, lots were drawn to determine which of the newly elected members would serve a four-year term, with the other three serving two-year terms. A city manager is responsible for the city's executive functions, managing Cape May's activities and operation.[[7]](#cite_note-DataBook-7)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) Voters approved a November 2010 referendum to shift the city's elections from May to November, with city officials estimating that the change would save $30,000 in costs that had been associated with each May election.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

In March 2015, Councilman Jerry Inderwies Jr. resigned to protest what he called a "witch hunt" against the police chief.[[88]](#cite_note-88) In the November 2015 general election, Roger Furlin was elected to fill the balance of the council seat vacated by Inderwies.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

In January 2021, the city council selected Lorraine Baldwin to fill the council seat expiring in 2022 that had been held by Zachary Mullock until he resigned to take office as mayor.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Baldwin served on an interim basis until the November 2021 general election, when voters chose her to serve the balance of the term of office.[[91]](#cite_note-CapeMay2021-91) Also in January 2021, Michael Voll was appointed to City Manager.

In November 2021, the city council appointed Michael Yeager to fill the seat expiring in December 2021 that had been held by Christopher Bezaire until he resigned after pleading guilty earlier that month to charges that he had engaged in stalking an ex-girlfriend and that he had been in contempt of court.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the Mayor of Cape May City is Zachary Mullock, whose term of office ends December 31, 2028. Other members of the Cape May City Council are Deputy Mayor Maureen K. McDade (2026), Lorraine M. Baldwin (2026), Shaine P. Meier (2026), and Steve Bodnar (2028).[[3]](#cite_note-Council-3)[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-CapeMayOfficials-94)[[95]](#cite_note-CapeMay2022-95)[[91]](#cite_note-CapeMay2021-91)[[96]](#cite_note-CapeMay2020-96)

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Cape_May_Fire_Station_%28current%29.jpg/250px-Cape_May_Fire_Station_%28current%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Fire_Station_(current).jpg)

Current Cape May fire station

Cape May established a desalinization plant in the late 1990s to manage salt going into its water aquifers.[[133]](#cite_note-Jordanp116-133)

Cape May's current sewage plant in 1960 or 1961, less than a year after the [New Jersey Attorney General](/wiki/New_Jersey_Attorney_General "New Jersey Attorney General")'s deadline for Cape May Point to have a sewage plant, as it had previously dumped sewage in the [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay"); the [New Jersey Department of Health](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Health "New Jersey Department of Health") had warned the borough about this in 1951. Despite the borough missing the deadline, the state never fined the borough as the Attorney General removed his judgment.[[134]](#cite_note-Jordanp117-134)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/CapeMayESNJ.JPG/330px-CapeMayESNJ.JPG)](/wiki/File:CapeMayESNJ.JPG)

[Cape May City Elementary School](/wiki/Cape_May_City_Elementary_School "Cape May City Elementary School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Cape_May_Library_%28former_Franklin_Street_School%29_02.jpg/330px-Cape_May_Library_%28former_Franklin_Street_School%29_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Library_(former_Franklin_Street_School)_02.jpg)

Franklin Street School, a former [elementary school for black children](/wiki/Black_school "Black school") and the current site of the Cape May Library

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/FormerOurLadyStaroftheSeaSchoolCapeMayNJ.jpg/330px-FormerOurLadyStaroftheSeaSchoolCapeMayNJ.jpg)](/wiki/File:FormerOurLadyStaroftheSeaSchoolCapeMayNJ.jpg)

The former Our Lady Star of the Sea Catholic School, which merged into what later became [Wildwood Catholic Academy](/wiki/Wildwood_Catholic_Academy "Wildwood Catholic Academy") in 2010

For [pre-kindergarten](/wiki/Pre-kindergarten "Pre-kindergarten") through [sixth grade](/wiki/Sixth_grade "Sixth grade"), public school students attend Cape May City Elementary School as part of the [Cape May City School District](/wiki/Cape_May_City_School_District "Cape May City School District").[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) As of the 2024–25 school year, the district, comprised of one school, had an enrollment of 151 students and 25.0 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 6.1:1.[[138]](#cite_note-NCES-138) Also attending are students from [Cape May Point](/wiki/Cape_May_Point,_New_Jersey "Cape May Point, New Jersey"), a non-operating district, as part of a [sending/receiving relationship](/wiki/Sending/receiving_relationship "Sending/receiving relationship"), with most students in the district coming from the [United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May").[[139]](#cite_note-139)

For [seventh](/wiki/Seventh_grade "Seventh grade") through [twelfth grades](/wiki/Twelfth_grade "Twelfth grade"), public school students attend the schools of the [Lower Cape May Regional School District](/wiki/Lower_Cape_May_Regional_School_District "Lower Cape May Regional School District"), which serves students from Cape May, [Lower Township](/wiki/Lower_Township,_New_Jersey "Lower Township, New Jersey") and [West Cape May](/wiki/West_Cape_May,_New_Jersey "West Cape May, New Jersey"), along with students from [Cape May Point](/wiki/Cape_May_Point,_New_Jersey "Cape May Point, New Jersey") who attend as part of a [sending/receiving relationship](/wiki/Sending/receiving_relationship "Sending/receiving relationship").[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-Study-141) Schools in the district (with 2024–25 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[142]](#cite_note-142)) are
Richard M. Teitelman Middle School[[143]](#cite_note-143) with 394 students in grades 7–8 and
[Lower Cape May Regional High School](/wiki/Lower_Cape_May_Regional_High_School "Lower Cape May Regional High School")[[144]](#cite_note-144) with 717 students in grades 9–12.[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146) The high school district's [board of education](/wiki/Board_of_education "Board of education") is comprised of nine members, who are elected directly by voters to serve three-year terms of office on a staggered basis, with three seats up for election each year.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148) Seats on the board are allocated based on population, with Cape May assigned one seat.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

Students are also eligible to attend [Cape May County Technical High School](/wiki/Cape_May_County_Technical_High_School "Cape May County Technical High School") in Cape May Court House, which serves students from the entire county in its comprehensive and vocational programs, which are offered without charge to students who are county residents.[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) Special needs students may be referred to [Cape May County Special Services School District](/wiki/Cape_May_County_Special_Services_School_District "Cape May County Special Services School District") in the Cape May Court House area.

The nearest private Catholic school serving Cape May is [Wildwood Catholic Academy](/wiki/Wildwood_Catholic_Academy "Wildwood Catholic Academy") (Pre-K12) in [North Wildwood](/wiki/North_Wildwood,_New_Jersey "North Wildwood, New Jersey"), under the auspices of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Camden "Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden").

Colleges and universities in the Cape May area include [Atlantic Cape Community College](/wiki/Atlantic_Cape_Community_College "Atlantic Cape Community College"), [Rutgers University–Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Camden "Rutgers University–Camden"), and the Institute of Marine and Coastal Sciences.

The Cape May Branch of the Cape May County Public Library is located in Cape May City.[[152]](#cite_note-152) The library was previously in city hall but later moved to a standalone building. In 2009 an estimated $507,800 renovation was to take place with $395,300, or about 78% of the expenses, paid by Cape May County.[[153]](#cite_note-153) In 2024 it moved from a previous location to the renovated Franklin Street School.[[154]](#cite_note-154) A task force convened by Cape May City Council stated that the former library on Ocean Street should be used as a community center.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

### History of education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: History of education")]

According to an 1868 article in *[The Inkwell](/wiki/The_Inkwell "The Inkwell")* by William Lycett, historically Cape May had a school known as the "Indian Queen.", until another school opened in 1868. He also stated that his father operated a private educational institution.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

The first Cape May High School, built in 1901, was designed by Seymour Davis and built for $35,000.[[157]](#cite_note-Pochersp-157) In 1917 a new Cape May High School facility was built,[[158]](#cite_note-Barlowpubsafbldg-158) with the 1901 building becoming an elementary school.[[157]](#cite_note-Pochersp-157) In the past Cape May elementary schools were [segregated on the basis of race](/wiki/Educational_segregation_in_the_United_States "Educational segregation in the United States"); churches and households initially educated black children. From 1928 to 1948, black elementary school students attended Franklin Street School.[[159]](#cite_note-159) Cape May High School educated students of all races.[[160]](#cite_note-160) Cape May High closed effective December 22, 1960, and LCMRHS opened in 1961.[[161]](#cite_note-161)c. 1970 the first Cape May High School building was demolished, and was replaced with an [Acme Markets](/wiki/Acme_Markets "Acme Markets") location that occupied the site starting in the 1970s.[[157]](#cite_note-Pochersp-157) The second Cape May High School building has since become the city hall and police station.[[158]](#cite_note-Barlowpubsafbldg-158)

Cape May previously had its own Catholic [K–8 school](/wiki/K%E2%80%938_school "K–8 school"), Our Lady Star of the Sea School, which served as the parish school for Our Lady Star of the Sea, St. John of God ([North Cape May](/wiki/North_Cape_May "North Cape May")) and St. Raymond ([Villas](/wiki/Villas,_New_Jersey "Villas, New Jersey")) churches.[[162]](#cite_note-162) The St. Raymond School closed in 2007 with students sent to Our Lady Star of the Sea.[[163]](#cite_note-Ianeri-163) In 2010 Our Lady Star of the Sea merged into Cape Trinity Regional School (Pre-K–8) in North Wildwood.[[164]](#cite_note-DiPasquale-164) That school in turn merged into Wildwood Catholic Academy in 2020.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

Starting in 2010, discussions were under way regarding a possible consolidation of the districts of Cape May City, Cape May Point and the [West Cape May School District](/wiki/West_Cape_May_Elementary_School "West Cape May Elementary School").[[166]](#cite_note-166)

The Franklin Street School opened as the current library due to a renovation worth $11,000,000. About one and one half years was the duration of the project completion. The opening ceremony involved a chain of people moving books between the old and new libraries with their hands.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

### Roads and highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Roads and highways")]

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the city had a total of 31.63 mi (50.90 km) of roadways, of which 24.99 mi (40.22 km) were maintained by the municipality and 6.64 mi (10.69 km) by Cape May County.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

[Route 109](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_109 "New Jersey Route 109") leads into Cape May from the north and provides access to the southern terminus of the [Garden State Parkway](/wiki/Garden_State_Parkway "Garden State Parkway") along with [U.S. Route 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 9 in New Jersey") in neighboring Lower Township.[[169]](#cite_note-169) U.S. Route 9 leads to the [Cape May–Lewes Ferry](/wiki/Cape_May%E2%80%93Lewes_Ferry "Cape May–Lewes Ferry"), which heads across the [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") to [Lewes, Delaware](/wiki/Lewes,_Delaware "Lewes, Delaware").[[170]](#cite_note-170)

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Cape_May_Station_and_Welcome_Center.jpg/330px-Cape_May_Station_and_Welcome_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_May_Station_and_Welcome_Center.jpg)

[Cape May Station](/wiki/Cape_May_Station "Cape May Station") and Welcome Center (bus station and former train station)

[NJ Transit](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") provides service to [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") on the [313](/wiki/313_(New_Jersey_bus) "313 (New Jersey bus)") and [315](/wiki/315_(New_Jersey_bus) "315 (New Jersey bus)") routes and to [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey") on the [552](/wiki/552_(New_Jersey_bus) "552 (New Jersey bus)") route, with seasonal service to Philadelphia on the [316](/wiki/316_(New_Jersey_bus) "316 (New Jersey bus)") route and to the [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") on the [319](/wiki/319_(New_Jersey_bus) "319 (New Jersey bus)") route.[[171]](#cite_note-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172)

The Great American Trolley Company operates trolley service in Cape May daily during the summer months, running along a loop route through the city.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

The city is served by rail from the [Cape May City Rail Terminal](/wiki/Cape_May_City_Rail_Terminal "Cape May City Rail Terminal"), offering [excursion train](/wiki/Excursion_train "Excursion train") service on the [Cape May Seashore Lines](/wiki/Cape_May_Seashore_Lines "Cape May Seashore Lines") from the terminal located at the intersection of Lafayette Street and Elmira Street.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)

The city last had regional passenger train service by the [Pennsylvania-Reading Seashore Lines](/wiki/Pennsylvania-Reading_Seashore_Lines "Pennsylvania-Reading Seashore Lines") in the mid-1960s. Final service into [Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey") (across the Delaware River from [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")) ended in January 1966, while service to [Lindenwold station](/wiki/Lindenwold_station "Lindenwold station") ended in October 1981.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

### Ferry transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ferry transport")]

The [Delaware River and Bay Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_and_Bay_Authority "Delaware River and Bay Authority") operates the [Cape May-Lewes Ferry](/wiki/Cape_May-Lewes_Ferry "Cape May-Lewes Ferry") year-round, a 70-85 minute across [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") to [Lewes, Delaware](/wiki/Lewes,_Delaware "Lewes, Delaware"), carrying passengers and cars.[[177]](#cite_note-177) The ferry constitutes a portion of [U.S. Route 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9 "U.S. Route 9").

The Delaware River and Bay Authority operates a shuttle bus in the summer months which connects the Cape May Welcome Center with the Cape May–Lewes Ferry terminal.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

## Coast Guard Training Center Cape May

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Coast Guard Training Center Cape May")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/USCoastGuardTrainingCenterCapeMay.JPG/330px-USCoastGuardTrainingCenterCapeMay.JPG)](/wiki/File:USCoastGuardTrainingCenterCapeMay.JPG)

The [U.S. Coast Guard Training Center in Cape May](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May")

The [United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Training_Center_Cape_May "United States Coast Guard Training Center Cape May"), New Jersey is the nation's only Coast Guard Recruit Training Center. In 1924, the U.S. Coast Guard occupied the base and established air facilities for planes used in support of [United States Customs Service](/wiki/United_States_Customs_Service "United States Customs Service") efforts. During the [Prohibition](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Eighteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") era, several cutters were assigned to Cape May to foil rumrunners operating off the New Jersey coast. After Prohibition, the Coast Guard all but abandoned Cape May leaving a small air/sea rescue contingent. For a short period of time (1929–1934), part of the base was used as a civilian airport. With the advent of World War II, a larger airstrip was constructed and the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") returned to train [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") pilots. The over the water approach simulated carrier landings at sea. The Coast Guard also increased its Cape May forces for coastal patrol, anti-submarine warfare, air/sea rescue and buoy service. In 1946, the Navy relinquished the base to the Coast Guard.[[181]](#cite_note-181) The [Cape May Airport](/wiki/Cape_May_Airport "Cape May Airport") still houses the [Naval Air Station Wildwood Aviation Museum](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Wildwood_Aviation_Museum "Naval Air Station Wildwood Aviation Museum").

In 1948, all entry-level training on the [U.S. East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") was moved to the U.S. Coast Guard Recruit Receiving Station in Cape May. The [U.S. Coast Guard](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard "United States Coast Guard") consolidated all recruit training functions in Cape May in 1982. Over 350 military and civilian personnel and their dependents are attached to the Cape May Training Center.[[182]](#cite_note-182)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_May,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* Cape May is the subject of the song "[On the Way to Cape May](/wiki/On_the_Way_to_Cape_May "On the Way to Cape May")", originally sung by [Cozy Morley](/wiki/Cozy_Morley "Cozy Morley").[[183]](#cite_note-183)
* The 1980s horror film *[The Prowler](/wiki/The_Prowler_(1981_film) "The Prowler (1981 film)")* was filmed entirely on location in Cape May.[[184]](#cite_note-184)
* The town lends its name to the Cape May Cafe, a restaurant in the [Beach Club Resort](/wiki/Disney%27s_Beach_Club_Resort "Disney's Beach Club Resort") at [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World_Resort "Walt Disney World Resort").[[185]](#cite_note-185)
* In *[The Blacklist](/wiki/The_Blacklist "The Blacklist")*, Cape May is the setting in the episode "Cape May".[[186]](#cite_note-186)
* Scenes for the film *[A Complete Unknown](/wiki/A_Complete_Unknown "A Complete Unknown")*, a biopic about [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") featuring [Timothée Chalamet](/wiki/Timoth%C3%A9e_Chalamet "Timothée Chalamet"), were filmed in Cape May in the spring of 2024. Cape May served as a suitable location to mimic [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island") and the 1965 [Newport Folk Festival](/wiki/Newport_Folk_Festival "Newport Folk Festival"), where Dylan first performed in public with an electric guitar. Minimal redress was needed, given the resort's commitment to its designation as a National Historic Landmark, with its concentration of Victorian architecture as well as other 19th and 20th century architectural motifs.[[187]](#cite_note-187)
* On 8/28/2025 the single "Ode to Cape May," A song by [Nikki Fournier](/w/index.php?title=Nikki_Fournier&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nikki Fournier (page does not exist)") was released by [Partridge Records and Music, LLC](/w/index.php?title=Partridge_Records_and_Music,_LLC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Partridge Records and Music, LLC (page does not exist)")[[188]](#cite_note-188)
